Customer Experience Systems Case Study

The Aethel

For eight years, I was on the other side of this system — fielding the calls that should never have happened. This is what I built when I decided to fix it.

Background
8 Years Hospitality Operations · NYC · London · São Paulo
Format
Concept · Solo Project · Mobile PWA
Focus
Service Design · Systems Thinking · UX / UI
Discovery
13,644 support tickets · Empire Hotel New York · Aug–Sep 2025 · One issue type
01 Operational Context

This problem has a paper trail.

Eight years. Four properties. Three countries. The same breakdown, every time.

Not every request fails. Most are fulfilled — towels delivered, repairs completed, items retrieved. The operation, technically, works.

What kept failing was the moment between dispatch and arrival. The guest submits a request. The front desk logs it. The system carries it forward. And from that point on — the guest is alone with uncertainty.

They call back. We answer. We say "it's on its way." We don't actually know if it is. We check with housekeeping. Housekeeping checks with the runner. Someone says five minutes. It's been twenty.

This is not a failure of people. It's a failure of visibility — baked into every property management system I've worked with. The requests, in most cases, were being fulfilled. The breakdown was informational — and that distinction changes everything about what the solution needs to be.

"In hospitality, the experience breaks before the service fails — it breaks the moment the guest no longer understands what is happening."

I didn't build this because it seemed interesting. I built it after pulling a support ticket log and finding 13,644 entries filtered to a single issue type — repeated contacts, same rooms, consecutive days. Each one a guest who called back because the system had the information and never surfaced it. That's not a service problem. That's a recoverable operational cost that compounds daily.

02 Why This Matters

This is not a UX problem.

Support ticket system, The Empire Hotel New York · Aug–Sep 2025 · Filtered: AC Issue · Total: 13,644 · Names and room numbers redacted

Property Management System color-coding schema — internal guest status tiers. The system coded who to prioritize. It did not tell the guest anything. Guest data redacted.

There's a moment I watched happen dozens of times. A guest walks up to the front desk — not to check in, not to check out. They walk up because they submitted a request forty minutes ago and heard nothing.

They're not angry yet. They're uncertain. And uncertainty, at this level of hospitality, reads as neglect.

The conversation that follows takes three minutes. It involves one front desk agent, one radio call to housekeeping, a wait, a vague answer, and a promise. The guest walks away — maybe reassured, maybe not. Three guests behind them in line watched the whole thing.

That interaction did not need to happen. The request was being fulfilled. The team member was on the way. The only thing missing was a number on a screen.

This is why visibility is not a UX problem. It's a service integrity problem. Every unnecessary escalation is a public moment — witnessed by other guests, absorbed into their perception of the property, reflected in the review they write six days later.

"The goal was never to speed up the service. It was to make the wait feel like service."

In hospitality, the damage lands in a public review. In a product environment, it surfaces as a CSAT score, an NPS detractor, or a renewal conversation that starts from a position of eroded trust.

03 Stress Scenarios

Where the System Breaks Under Pressure

Four moments where the experience fails — not because execution broke, but because the system stopped communicating. Each maps directly to failure patterns in any high-touch client environment.

Risk · Silent Failure
High Demand

When capacity is high, requests lose clarity. The guest cannot distinguish between queued, delayed, or lost. Silence, at this level, is interpreted as failure — regardless of what is actually happening.

Decision
Expose constraint clearly — name it, estimate it, commit to updating it
Outcome: Honest delay outperforms false reassurance every time
Screen 07 — High Demand
Risk · False Promise
In-Progress Cancellation

When a request is already in motion, cancellation becomes ambiguous. A binary yes/no response creates a false sense of control. The system must communicate operational constraint — not certainty.

Decision
Explain the coordination required before confirming any outcome
Outcome: Trust maintained without fabricated certainty
Screens 08 + 10-A — Cancel Modal & Pending
Risk · Closed-but-Open
Unresolved Resolution

A request can be marked complete while the issue still exists. Without a resolution check, the failure disappears from the system — and returns as a public escalation that is harder to contain.

Decision
Require guest confirmation and surface a reopen path
Outcome: Catch hidden failures before they reach the lobby
Screens 09-A + 09-C — Completion & Reopen
Risk · Rejected Input
When the System Cannot Comply

When the guest asks for something the system operationally cannot do, the design choice is binary: hide the constraint or name it and redirect. Every rejection is either a trust failure or a trust signal.

Decision
Explain the "no," reveal what already happened, offer the next valid path
Outcome: Rejection becomes redirection — the guest stays in-system
Screen 10-C — Cancellation Rejected (three states)
04 Strategy

Reframing the Problem

The instinct is to build faster. Reduce the wait time. Optimize the queue. That would solve latency. It would not solve the problem.

The real constraint was not time — it was uncertainty. A guest who waits 20 minutes with a visible ETA feels more in control than one who waits 10 minutes in silence. The product needed to reduce perceived wait, not just actual wait.

01

Status reaches the guest — the guest doesn't chase status. Every update is pushed, not pulled. The moment a guest still needs to call back after submitting a request, the system has already failed.

02

Because status is visible, every state needs a visible next step. "In progress" is not a status — it's a void. The guest needs to know what in progress means: who has it, where they are, when to expect them.

03

Because next steps are visible, constraints must be visible too. When capacity is high, the system doesn't say "processing." It says there is high demand, here is the estimated wait, here is what happens next. Operational truth over optimism.

04

Because constraints are named honestly, the front desk becomes the fallback — not the first resort. If a guest taps "Contact front desk" while their request is live and visible, that is a product failure. Not a service recovery moment.

05 Architectural Decisions

The Decision Matrix

Each row is not a feature. It is a decision. Every entry connects a specific operational friction to a system response — and maps that response to a strategic outcome. The goal was not to build a feature list. It was to remove the customer's need to follow up, one friction point at a time.

Operational Friction System Decision Strategic Outcome
Opaque handoffs Centralized request tracking Shared state-truth across guest and staff
Guest anxiety during wait Real-time ETA & status timeline Eliminates the need for manual desk follow-ups
Input paralysis at entry Structured UI + inline fallback text High-speed entry that absorbs categorization errors
Forgotten status updates Task-tied state changes Removes reliance on staff remembering to update
Hardware / download barriers QR-based PWA framework Zero-download access with immediate utility
Guest input the system cannot fulfill Honest rejection + redirect Trust maintained through transparency, not silence
Scope Boundaries — What the System Deliberately Does Not Do
No native app

QR-based PWA removes the download barrier.

Sacrificed: push notifications on iOS. Gained: zero-friction entry a guest will actually use.

No full cancellation

Once in progress, the system manages constraint honestly across three outcomes: confirmed, rejected, or abandoned.

None apologizes. All explain.

No full automation

Staff-triggered transitions preserve human judgment at dispatch.

GPS infrastructure is unreliable across properties; automation creates a different class of failure.

06 System Behavior

Synchronizing Frontstage and Backstage

Phase 01 · Initiation Phase 02 · Processing Phase 03 · Dispatch Phase 04 · Resolution Phase 05 · Exception
Guest Submits request via triage UI Receives dynamic ETA anchor Tracks live timeline updates Confirms and rates resolution Attempts cancellation or reopen
System Ticket generated & timestamped Category assigned via routing logic State transitions triggered by staff action¹ Feedback captured for CSAT Checks operational state → routes to Pending, Confirmed, or Rejected
Staff Assignment received in queue Accepts and initiates task Updates progress state in real time Marks operational completion Confirms or rejects cancellation within 2 min

¹ State transitions are staff-triggered, not automated — GPS infrastructure is unreliable across properties, and removing human judgment from dispatch creates a different class of failure. · State fields per request: request_id · category · assigned_to · current_status · eta_timestamp

07 Interface

Six Behaviors. Eighteen States. One System.

Each group of screens is a behavior — not a feature set. The value of this interface is not in the ideal path. It is in how the system performs when things are not ideal.

Ch.1
Access
2 states
Ch.2
Request Formation
3 states
Ch.3
Live Visibility
3 states
Ch.4
Cancellation
3 states
Ch.5
Resolution Integrity
3 states
Ch.6
Rejection as Redirection
4 states
Ch.1 · Access
Entry without mediation.
"Error states named before they become escalations."
01 · Access — Default

QR-based entry removes staff mediation entirely. The guest enters the system before asking for permission.

02 · Access — Error State

"We couldn't find your reservation" is specific. "Something went wrong" is not. The error names the problem so the guest knows whether to retry or call the desk.

Decision: Specific error copy over generic fallback
Prevents: Front desk call for a resolvable input error
Takeaway

The guest enters the system before the hotel knows they need help. Failure states are specific enough to resolve without front desk contact.

Ch.2 · Request Formation
Ambiguity absorbed at intake.
"Expectation set before the clock starts."
03 · New Request — Triage

Three categories cover ~90% of requests. The free-text fallback is a deliberate routing mechanism — ambiguous requests are better captured in the guest's words than misrouted by forced categorization.

04 · Specify Request

"Something else" is not a failure state — it's an honest option. Absorbing ambiguity here costs nothing. Misrouting costs minutes and a follow-up.

05 · Confirm Request

The ETA appears before submission — not after. A guest who sees "10–15 minutes" before confirming has calibrated their expectation before the clock starts.

Decision: ETA at confirmation, not post-submission
Outcome: Expectation is set before measuring begins
Takeaway

Misrouting costs minutes and a follow-up. Absorbing ambiguity at intake costs nothing. The ETA before submission means expectation is calibrated before the guest starts measuring.

Ch.3 · Live Visibility
Progress visible = follow-up unnecessary.
"Constraint named = trust maintained."
06 · Active Tracking

The live timeline shows the guest that the system is tracking. "Contact front desk" is always visible, but unmistakably the fallback.

Decision: Visible progress states
Outcome: Fewer status calls to the front desk
06-R · Tracking — Reopened

Reuse Screen 06 with a context banner — the guest isn't starting over. The timeline preserves every prior event.

Prevents: Disorientation after a reopen
07 · High Demand

The system names the constraint clearly — approximate wait, reason, and commitment to update. Honest disappointment outperforms false reassurance every time.

Decision: Expose capacity constraint explicitly
Outcome: Reduced false expectations → fewer escalations
Takeaway

Tracking, reopen, and high demand cover the most common reasons a guest picks up the phone. When the system is visible, the phone stays down.

Ch.4 · Cancellation Under Constraint
The system doesn't pretend control it doesn't have.
07-C · Early Cancel — Pre-Dispatch

Before any team member is assigned, cancellation is immediate. The modal reflects this honestly — no "we'll check with the team" theater when there's no team to check with.

08 · Cancel Modal — In Progress

"We'll check with the assigned team" is not a limitation disguised as a feature. It's an honest communication of operational constraint — the system explains why instead of pretending the guest has control they don't.

10-A · Cancellation Pending

Active waiting state, not silence. The guest does not assume the system lost their request. 2-minute window with one escape: go back.

Takeaway

Pre-dispatch and in-progress cancellations are operationally different situations. The system communicates that difference honestly — no theater, no fake control.

Ch.5 · Resolution Integrity
Completion only exists when the guest confirms it.
09-A · Completion Confirmation

Completion requires guest confirmation — not just a staff tap. "Yes, it's resolved" closes the loop cleanly. "Something's still missing" surfaces the gap before it becomes a lobby escalation.

09-B · Resolved & Rated

Full timeline visible after completion. Transparency after delivery builds the same trust as transparency during it.

Decision: Full history visible post-resolution
Rationale: Accountability doesn't end at delivery
09-C · Reopen Request

One tap surfaces the gap. "Contact front desk directly" is the last option — the system has one more chance to contain the escalation before it becomes a public lobby moment.

Takeaway

A request marked complete by staff is not the same as a request resolved for the guest. The system holds that distinction — and gives the guest one more path before the escalation reaches the lobby.

Ch.6 · Rejection as Redirection
When the system cannot comply, it does not disappear.
"It explains, redirects, and preserves trust."
Typical System Behavior
  • 01Generic error with no context
  • 02What was attempted is not acknowledged
  • 03No next step offered
  • 04Guest exits to front desk — trust lost
The Aethel
  • 01Explain the outcome directly and calmly
  • 02Reveal what already happened
  • 03Offer the next valid path
  • 04Guest stays in-system — trust preserved
10-B · Request Cancelled

Grey icon — not red, not teal. Closed state, not failed state. Copy follows the same logic: factual, not apologetic.

Prevents: Emotional dramatization of a guest-initiated action
10-C·A · Cancellation Rejected

Lead with resolution, not rejection. Gold icon — timing collision, not failure. Prevents the guest interpreting a fulfilled request as a system error.

10-C·1 · Still Not Right

If something was actually wrong with the delivery, the system doesn't close the loop — it asks "What wasn't right?" and opens a new request immediately. The rejection becomes an intake.

10-C·2 · Closed with Full Context

The timeline includes the cancellation attempt — shown in gold italic as a contextual event, not an error. The system tracked what actually happened, including the moment the guest tried to exit.

Two Deliberate Decisions

The rejection icon is gold — not red. Red signals failure. Gold signals a timing collision with an outcome that is actually okay. The cancellation attempt appears in the final timeline because the system tracks reality, not just the happy path.

Principle

"When the system cannot comply, it does not disappear. It explains, redirects, and stays visible."

08 Business & Experience Impact

Shifting the System

The system was designed to intercept the conditions that produce repetition — not by speeding up execution, but by making state visible before the guest needs to ask.

13,644
Support tickets · One property · Several weeks · One issue type

Filtered to a single issue type, the log returned dozens of repeated entries across consecutive days, for the same rooms, from different guests. Each repeat entry represents a guest who contacted the front desk a second time. Or a third.

A status call takes roughly 3 minutes: the guest explains, the agent checks, someone radios the team, a response comes back. At a property handling 80 requests per shift, even a 15% callback rate means nearly two full hours of front desk capacity spent on information the system already had — and never surfaced.

That capacity isn't recovered through faster service. It's recovered through visible service. The problem was never that the operation was slow. The problem was that it was invisible.

In a SaaS environment, the same pattern surfaces as elevated support tickets during onboarding, extended time-to-value, and — if unaddressed — a churn signal that looks like disengagement but is actually confusion. The customer didn't leave because the product failed. They left because the system stopped telling them what was happening.

Design Hypothesis for V1

These are projected targets — not measured results. This is a concept. The numbers below represent the design intent and are grounded in existing hospitality research, not in deployment data. A hiring manager who asks "did you measure this?" should receive that answer directly.

Projected · Not Measured

Targets for V1 based on research + operational logic

Metric Target Rationale
Reduction in secondary status calls 40% Grounded in hospitality research showing 30–50% call reduction when guests have real-time request visibility. Mid-range target for V1.
Tickets triggering automated state updates 80% Accounts for partial staff adoption and legacy workflow habits. Ambitious but achievable without full operational change management.
Abandonment to "Contact front desk" during active tracking <15% Each point below this represents a guest who trusted the system over the phone.
09 Reflection

What I Would Test Next

V1 proves the hypothesis: legibility reduces friction. What it does not yet answer is how the system performs when it is right about the process but wrong about the timing — and what happens when the staff side of the loop fails silently.

Test 01

Does proactive delay notification preserve trust better than silent ETA drift? A guest who sees "Arriving in 8 minutes" and waits 20 may experience worse damage than one who never saw the ETA.

Test 02

How does the system detect when staff-side state transitions fail? If a team member marks a request done without triggering the workflow, the guest sees "completed" while the issue persists.

Test 03

Is a 2-minute cancellation response window experienced as honest or slow? The system commits to this — but it's a design assumption, not a measured baseline.

"V1 makes the system legible. V2 needs to make it trustworthy when it's wrong — and honest about when it can't be."

This case study is a product. Designed in Figma, built in code, versioned on GitHub, published via Vercel. The medium follows the same standard as the system it documents.
